title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a guide called Anna talking about volunteering at a historic castle. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a guide called Anna talking about volunteering at a historic castle. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A guide called Anna describes volunteering at a historic castle.
extracts.audio_script: Hello everyone. I’m Anna, one of the guides at Stonebridge Castle, and I’m going to describe the ways volunteers can help us. The castle itself is over six hundred years old, although some buildings around it are much newer. Our visitor reception is not inside a medieval hall, as tourists sometimes believe. It occupies a converted [9]farm cottage[/9] near the entrance gate, where tickets are sold and visitors can leave bags before exploring. Last year more than fifty thousand people visited, so we depend heavily on volunteers.||Stonebridge has several exhibitions. Visitors enjoy seeing old furniture and clothing, but at present the part producing the most questions is our display about [10]castle kitchens[/10], because people are fascinated by how meals were prepared without modern equipment. All volunteers receive training, whether they plan to work with visitors or behind the scenes. The first session includes the history of the building, but its main purpose is to explain [11]visitor assistance[/11], including how to answer basic questions and when to ask a member of staff for help.||New volunteers are never expected to deliver full tours immediately. In their first few weeks, most work at the [12]entrance gate[/12], welcoming ticket holders, showing where the route begins and checking that large bags are left safely. We provide a badge and a light jacket. As parts of the castle have uneven stone floors and steep steps, volunteers are asked to wear [13]flat shoes[/13], even if they are only expecting to work indoors. Comfort and safety are more important than looking formal.||We are always improving the experience for visitors. This year, an unused upstairs room has become a [14]costume gallery[/14], displaying copies of historic clothing which can be seen clearly without damaging original material. Volunteers will be trained to assist there. Children visiting with schools take part in activities as well as tours. The new school activity which teachers requested most often is [15]writing with quills[/15], where pupils try forming letters using safe modern versions of historic pens. It provides a memorable link with the documents they see during the visit.||Volunteers wishing to learn more can attend monthly talks. The first one to fill this season was about [16]garden archaeology[/16], which explores what historic plants and paths can tell us. Volunteers completing twenty-five hours receive a free [17]annual pass[/17], allowing them to bring a guest on later visits. Our major goal is to open more of the castle to everyone. Ticket income from a special evening event in autumn will help install a new [18]platform lift[/18] in part of the visitor route. Application forms are available at reception, and the next training day is in three weeks.

dialogue.speaker: anna
dialogue.text: Hello everyone. I’m Anna, one of the guides at Stonebridge Castle, and I’m going to describe the ways volunteers can help us. The castle itself is over six hundred years old, although some buildings around it are much newer. Our visitor reception is not inside a medieval hall, as tourists sometimes believe. It occupies a converted farm cottage near the entrance gate, where tickets are sold and visitors can leave bags before exploring. Last year more than fifty thousand people visited, so we depend heavily on volunteers.||Stonebridge has several exhibitions. Visitors enjoy seeing old furniture and clothing, but at present the part producing the most questions is our display about castle kitchens, because people are fascinated by how meals were prepared without modern equipment. All volunteers receive training, whether they plan to work with visitors or behind the scenes. The first session includes the history of the building, but its main purpose is to explain visitor assistance, including how to answer basic questions and when to ask a member of staff for help.||New volunteers are never expected to deliver full tours immediately. In their first few weeks, most work at the entrance gate, welcoming ticket holders, showing where the route begins and checking that large bags are left safely. We provide a badge and a light jacket. As parts of the castle have uneven stone floors and steep steps, volunteers are asked to wear flat shoes, even if they are only expecting to work indoors. Comfort and safety are more important than looking formal.||We are always improving the experience for visitors. This year, an unused upstairs room has become a costume gallery, displaying copies of historic clothing which can be seen clearly without damaging original material. Volunteers will be trained to assist there. Children visiting with schools take part in activities as well as tours. The new school activity which teachers requested most often is writing with quills, where pupils try forming letters using safe modern versions of historic pens. It provides a memorable link with the documents they see during the visit.||Volunteers wishing to learn more can attend monthly talks. The first one to fill this season was about garden archaeology, which explores what historic plants and paths can tell us. Volunteers completing twenty-five hours receive a free annual pass, allowing them to bring a guest on later visits. Our major goal is to open more of the castle to everyone. Ticket income from a special evening event in autumn will help install a new platform lift in part of the visitor route. Application forms are available at reception, and the next training day is in three weeks.
